data.content:
I can see you have an MSTest project with a failing test — `CalculateShipping_ExpressDelivery_Returns15` expects `15.00m` but the method returns `10.00m`.

I'll start `dotnet watch test` which will automatically re-run your tests whenever you save a file:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to use hot reload to iterate on their failing tests. I can see that the express delivery test expects `15.00m` but the implementation returns `10.00m`, so that test will fail.

To set up hot reload for tests, I'll use `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically when files change. Let me start that up for them.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-5079b17a7f2a42beb91bbb7429fdfe29 && dotnet watch test --no-restore 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for hot reload", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

data.reasoningText:
It seems like dotnet watch exited without showing test output. Let me try a different approach - use `dotnet watch test` without `--no-restore` or check if we need to restore first.

Actually, looking at the output again: "dotnet watch ⌚ Waiting for changes" then "dotnet watch ⌚ Exited" then "dotnet watch ⏳ Waiting for a file to change before restarting ..."

This might mean the tests ran but the output wasn't captured well. Let me stop this and try again with verbose output, or try `dotnet restore` first and then `dotnet watch test`.

Actually, it looks like the build is hung or very slow. Let me try a different approach - let me stop this and try running `dotnet watch test` without verbosity, or try to run the tests first to make sure they work.
